By reading chronicles, epics, folktales, poetry, drama, and modern prose, students will trace the evolution of Ukraine’s cultural identity, traditions, and politics across centuries of foreign domination, national revival, and the country’s current struggle for survival. The course emphasizes how literature reflects and shapes Ukraine’s historical experiences—from medieval Christianity through the Cossack times to Soviet repression and Russia’s current colonial war of aggression against Ukraine (as defined by the UN).

title: Elementary Vietnamese
description: This course will emphasize the development of practical communicative ability in Vietnamese along with basic cultural knowledge of Vietnam. This course is for students with no prior knowledge of Vietnamese. Native speakers of this language are not allowed to take this course.  The CLC classes are self-instructional and are best suited for highly motivated and disciplined students.

title: Intro Grad Stdy In Mus
description: An introduction to the basic bibliographic tools and research techniques in music. Offered fall semester.

title: Film Scoring
description: A study of the art of scoring music for films. The course will examine aesthetics of film scoring and apply it in this course. Offered fall semester.

title: 18th-Century Counterpoint II
description: The analysis and writing of fugue, variation, ground-bass, and canon.

title: Analysis of Tonal Music
description: Survey of recent analytical techniques for tonal repertoires with an emphasis on creating close readings of complete pieces.

title: Studio Tech Arrang Orchestrn
description: This course is an approach to arranging and orchestration in both jazz and commercial settings. The emphasis will be placed on writing for the studio orchestra, MIDI instrumentation, and vocal groups with a focus on the 4- to 6-piece group (jazz and commercial). Offered fall semester.

title: Advanced Arranging
description: Study of current techniques in arranging, including use of symmetrical scales and chords, pedal points, polytonality and planing techniques. Offered spring semester.

title: Electronic Music I
description: A survey of seminal works featuring electronic elements. Mastering the principles of recording, editing, signal processing, mixing, mastering, MIDI, sampling, and sound design. This course encourages work that overlaps with other programs of study; particularly composition, performance, audio engineering, and music production.

title: Electronic Music II
description: A continued survey of seminal works featuring electronic elements. Instruction in the design and utilization of sound-generating computer programs for digital synthesis, MIDI, and advanced techniques associated with recording and producing audio. This course encourages work that overlaps with other programs of study; particularly, composition, performance, and audio engineering degrees.

title: Special Topics in Music Theory
description: Advanced course in music theory; topics may vary according to instructor.

title: Special Topics in Music Composition
description: Advanced course in music composition; topics may vary according to instructor.

title: Schenkerian Analysis
description: Study of pitch structure in tonal compositions as revealed by Heinrich Schenker and his followers.

title: Adv Composition I
description: Creation of large-scale musical compositions. Offered fall and spring semesters and on demand.

title: Adv Composition II
description: Creation of large-scale musical compositions. Offered fall and spring semesters and on demand.

title: Instrumentation
description: A survey of instrumental strengths, abilities, and weaknesses. Basic instrumentation and writing for all ensembles and orchestra. This course encourages work that overlaps with other programs of study; particularly composition, performance, audio engineering, and music production.

title: Studies In Special Lit
description: Materials vary each semester. May be repeated for credit up to four semesters.

title: Orchestration
description: A survey of instrumental strengths, abilities, and weaknesses. Special emphasis on the art and science of writing for larger ensembles. This course encourages work that overlaps with other programs of study; particularly composition, performance, audio engineering, and music production.

title: Graduate Diction Review
description: This course is an intensive review of undergraduate diction designed for graduate students who did not perform adequately on the Diction Diagnostic examination at matriculation.  A grade of no lower than C must be earned in this course if the student is to satisfy the requirement for mediation.

title: Renaissance Proseminar
description: An intensive study of the music, the composers, the diverse styles, genres, and techniques, the theoretical and aesthetic principles, and the performance practices associated with music of the Renaissance via reading, listening, and musical analysis.

title: Baroque Proseminar
description: An intensive study of the music, the composers, the diverse styles, genres, and techniques, the theoretical and aesthetic principles, and the performance practices associated with Baroque music via reading, listening, and musical analysis.

title: Prosem Opera History Lit
description: Survey of operatic styles from the 17th to the 20th centuries via discussion and analysis of selected works.

title: Analysis of 20th-Century Music
description: Survey of a variety of technical approaches to composition in the 20th century and such organizing techniques as harmony, voice leading, textural design, form, and orchestration.

title: Special Topics in Lyric Diction
description: This course is a rotation of special topics in advanced lyric diction designed for graduate students demonstrating basic proficiency in International Phonetic Alphabet usage as applied to lyric diction.

title: Music Theory Pedagogy
description: Music Theory Pedagogy provides students with opportunities to examine and implement theory pedagogy philosophies, curricular goals, and instructional approaches. Philosophically, students will consider central questions like “how does music theory help develop musical knowledge and skills?” and “what should an undergraduate music theory curriculum include?” The philosophical is balanced with the practical in this course: students learn and practice teaching methods by reading scholarship, studying published music theory curricula and textbooks, and taking on the role of teacher themselves. By exposing students to traditional and recent philosophies and methods of music theory pedagogy, Music Theory Pedagogy develops students’ knowledge and practical skills while creating space for reflection.

title: Graduate Vocal Pedagogy
description: A detailed study of the historical and contemporary methods of pedagogy, and analysis of pedagogical problems.

title: Prosem Music Classic Era
description: An intensive study of the music, the composers, the diverse styles and techniques, the theoretical and aesthetic principles, and the performance practices associated with music of the pre-classical and classical periods via reading, listening, and musical analysis.

title: Directed Studies Piano
description: Independent research projects in piano pedagogy. Offered each semester.

title: Prosem Music Romantic Pd
description: An intensive study of the music, the composers, the diverse styles and techniques, the theoretical and aesthetic principles, and the performance practices associated with music of the 19th century via reading, listening, and musical analysis.

title: Proseminar In Jazz
description: A study of jazz history and performance practices as applied to the performing musician and educator via transcription, analysis and research. Offered spring semester.

title: Prosem Music 20th Centry
description: An intensive study of the music, the composers, the diverse styles and techniques, the theoretical and aesthetic principles, and the performance practices associated with music composed after 1900 via reading, listening, and musical analysis.

title: Spec Topics Musicology
description: Advanced study of a selected topic in musicology. Offered on demand.

title: Hy Wind Band Trad Lit
description: Comprehensive study of band history from the Renaissance to the present, together with a survey of early and traditional wind works of Mozart, Mendelssohn, Berlioz, Holst, Grainger, Poulenc and others. Offered fall semesters and during the first summer terms of even-numbered years.

title: Projects In Wind Music
description: Survey of rehearsal techniques and studies in wind performance practices and transcription. Offered fall and spring semesters and during the first summer term.

title: Sem Wind Lit-Chambr Form
description: An analytical survey of the content and performance practices of selected wind ensemble literature for chamber groups.

title: Seminar in Orchestral Literature
description: An analytical survey of the content and performance practices of selected orchestral literature.

title: Choral Literature 1450-1750
description: In-depth survey of choral literature up to 1750. Offered spring semesters of odd-numbered years.

title: Choral Lit 1750-Present
description: In-depth survey of choral literature since 1750. Offered spring semesters of even-numbered years.

title: Graduate Vocal Literature I
description: In-depth survey of the performance criteria and historical significance of solo vocal literature of the United States, British Isles, Germany, Austria, France, Italy, Spain, and Central/South American. Offered in Alternate years.

title: Readings in Music History
description: Readings in Music History.

title: Adv Choral Conducting
description: Representative literature from all periods is studied and conducted. Conducting techniques, rehearsal and vocal techniques, diction, performance practice, and score analysis are emphasized. Offered each semester. May be repeated for credit with varied repertoire.

title: Adv Instrumental Conduct
description: Baton technique, score reading, analysis, interpretation, rehearsal techniques and instrumental problems in a few selected scores. Offered spring semester.

title: Graduate Theory Review
description: This course is a review and recasting of classical concepts, including harmony, counterpoint, and form.

title: Non-Thesis Research
description: No description available .

title: Thesis Research
description: This independent research course partially fulfills required master’s-level research thesis hours toward the master’s degree in music. The course is conducted under the guidance of the thesis advisor.  Material covered will be of an advanced nature aimed at providing master's students with an understanding of the latest research and current developments within the field. Discussion and advisor guidance will be directed towards readings of research articles and development of research methodology, with the aim of producing an original research contribution that represents a novel development in the field, or a novel perspective on a pre-existing topic in the field.

title: Seminar in Doctoral Research
description: This course builds upon the writing and research skills developed in courses like MUS 501 (Introduction to Graduate Study in Music), and it also serves as a prequel for the work students will undertake in MUS 699 (Document Research). It offers practical guidance in developing the various advanced projects required for the D.M.A. degree: the document prospectus, manuscript prospectus, and lecture/lecture-recital prospectus. The course additionally introduces best practices for crafting several written materials typically required for both academic and professional positions, including CVs/résumés, cover letters, and teaching/diversity statements. Situated at a transition point between students’ academic coursework and their culminating doctoral requirements, the course affords students the opportunity to apply their writing and research skills directly toward their future careers.

title: Writing About Music Theory
description: “Writing About Music Theory” offers students the opportunity to learn to read different genres of academic writing, including book reviews, articles, books, and dissertations/theses. Students dive deeply into music theory scholarship on a topic of their choice and hone their writing skills through intensive editing. By the end of the semester, students will have interacted with substantial amount of scholarship on their chosen topic and written a comprehensive literature review; and they will have several high-quality writing samples. This course will benefit students who seek to develop research projects and writing skills.

title: Adv Composition III
description: No description available .

title: Adv Composition IV
description: No description available .

title: Sem Medieval Renaiss Mus
description: An intensive study of a specific topic associated with Medieval and/or Renaissance music. Topic varies.

title: Seminar Romantic Music
description: An intensive study of a specific topic associated with 19th-century music. Topic varies.

title: Sem Twentieth Cent Music
description: An intensive study of a specific topic associated with music written in the 20th century. Topic varies.

title: Special Topics in Lyric Diction
description: This course is a rotation of special topics in advanced lyric diction designed for graduate students demonstrating basic proficiency in International Phonetic Alphabet usage as applied to lyric diction.

title: Opera Pedagogy and Stage Direction I
description: This two-course sequence is designed for graduate students interested in teaching opera stage craft and in opera stage direction.  Opera Pedagogy and Stage Direction I will primarily focus on administration and teaching within an Opera Workshop training and performing program.

title: Opera Pedagogy and Stage Direction II
description: This two-course sequence is designed for graduate students interested in teaching opera stage craft and in opera stage direction. Opera Pedagogy and Stage Direction II will primarily focus on stage direction and production administration.

title: Vocal Pedagogy
description: An analytical survey of voice teaching with emphasis on practical application. Offered on demand.

title: Brass Wdwind Strg Pedagy
description: Intended to teach doctoral students how to be successful in applied studio teaching at the collegiate and preparatory level. The focus of the course will be specific relative to the student's major instrument. Offered each semester.

title: Orchestral Conducting Pedagogy
description: A practical and theoretical study of conducting instruction.

title: Wind Conduct Pedagogy
description: A practical and theoretical study of conducting instruction.

title: Choral Conduct Pedagogy
description: A practical and theoretical study of conducting instruction.

title: Special Topics Vocal Lit I
description: In-depth survey of the performance criteria and historical significance of selected repertoire from the solo vocal literature.

title: Special Topics - Vocal Lit I
description: Students will acquire a basic working knowledge of standard solo art song by composers from Great Britain and France.

title: Sem in Wind Lit - Large
description: The Seminar in Wind Literature, Large Forms, focuses on a particular genre, composer or style period within the wind band repertoire.  The seminar for Spring 2014 will explore the music of David Maslanka.  Mr. Maslanka is considered to be one of the most significant composer of wind music over the later portion of the 20th century to the present.    The works to be studied were selected based on: an effort to include a representative sample of Maslanka’s complete catalogue, an effort to include representative types of his works (e.g. concerti, symphonies, etc.), an effort to include the works considered to be most significant, and the works intrinsic value.

title: Topics in Orchestral Literature
description: The Seminar in Orchestral Literature focuses on a particular genre, composer or style period within the Orchestral repertoire. The works to be studied are selected based on: an effort to include a representative sample of the selected composer’s complete catalogue, an effort to include representative types of works (e.g. concerti, symphonies, etc.), an effort to include the works considered to be most significant, and the works’ intrinsic value.

title: Topics Choral Literature
description: A thorough analysis and historical study of the repertoire for the lecture recital. May be repeated for credit.

title: Topics In Organ Lit
description: In-depth study of the organ literature of a specific period, its content and performance practice related to the organs of the period, and the performance of the literature. Offered each semester. May be repeated for credit if literature varies.

title: Adv Choral Conducting
description: Advanced conducting techniques, score analysis and preparation, performance practice and interpretation, rehearsal, and vocal techniques and diction.

title: Advanced Orchestral Conducting
description: Advanced baton technique, score reading, interpretation, rehearsal techniques and instrumental problems in selected scores.

title: Adv Wind Conducting
description: Advanced baton technique, score reading, interpretation, rehearsal techniques and instrumental problems in selected scores.

title: Non-Dissertatn Research
description: No description available .

title: Document Research
description: This independent research course partially fulfills required doctoral-level research dissertation hours toward the doctoral degree in music. The course is conducted under the guidance of the dissertation advisor.  Material covered will be of an advanced nature aimed at providing doctoral students with an understanding of the latest research and current developments within the field. Discussion and advisor guidance will be directed towards readings of research articles and development of research methodology, with the aim of producing an original research contribution that represents a novel development in the field, or a novel perspective on a pre-existing topic in the field.

title: Secondary Applied Study
description: Private instruction.

title: Early Chamber Ensembles
description: Early Chamber Ensembles is a small ensemble elective course open to graduate music majors upon audition. The Early Chamber Ensembles allow students to explore chamber music on period and modern instruments, learn historical playing and singing techniques, and hone interpretive skills in various Baroque and Classical styles.

title: Saxophone Ensemble
description: Applied Ensemble at the graduate level. This ensemble is open by audition to all University of Alabama graduate students.

title: Tuscaloosa-UA Community Chorus
description: The Tuscaloosa-UA Community Chorus is an auditioned choral ensemble that unites members of the University of Alabama School of Music and the greater Tuscaloosa community. This SATB choral ensemble celebrates and embraces the diversity of humanity through music in the form of solo choral performances and collaborative choral-orchestral performances. Members of this ensemble will engage with a wide variety of choral repertoire styles, composers, and historical eras representative of the diverse world in which our community exists.

title: Trumpet Ensemble
description: Applied performance ensemble for trumpet students at the graduate level.

title: Vocal Coaching
description: Small applied ensembles at the graduate level.

title: Horn Choir
description: Applied ensemble for horn students at the graduate level.

title: Tuba and Euphonium Ensemble
description: Small applied ensembles at the graduate level.

title: Huxford Symphony Orchestra
description: Applied ensemble at the graduate level.

title: Brass Choir
description: Applied ensemble for brass musicians. Permission of instructor is required.

title: Wind Ensemble
description: Applied Ensemble at the graduate level.

title: Symphonic Band
description: MUA 554 is a listening-focused, processed-oriented ensemble that provides an opportunity for the members to emphasize interactive musical skills in a large ensemble setting. Specifically on the graduate level, students would be interacting with both graduate and undergraduate musicians on current and historic works inside of the wind band repertoire. While promoting the advancement of wind music of artistic and historical significance, it reinforces the musical skills taught in the individual studios and develops the applications of those skills. Graduate students are pushed to embrace individual parts while interacting in a new ensemble setting full of student musicians with similar talents and skills.

title: Trombone Choir
description: Applied ensemble for trombone students at the graduate level.

title: Percussion Ensemble
description: Percussion ensemble at the graduate level.

title: Campus Band
description: Applied ensemble at the graduate level open to the entire campus community.

title: Contemporary Ensemble
description: Applied ensemble at the graduate level.

title: Jazz Ensembles
description: Applied ensemble in jazz for graduate students.

title: Opera Workshop
description: Applied ensemble at the graduate level.

title: Opera Production
description: This course will explore all aspects of opera production, including musical and staging rehearsals, technical elements and stage management and will culminate in a full length production.

title: University Chorus
description: Applied Ensemble at the Graduate level.

title: University Singers
description: Applied ensemble at the graduate level.

title: Alabama Chamber Choir
description: The Alabama Chamber Choir is a mixed choral ensemble primarily conducted by advanced University of Alabama undergraduate choral music education students.  Chamber Choir will afford all choristers the opportunity to peruse a broad spectrum of choral ensemble music, most of which will be suitable for programming in a traditional public school choral music setting.  While experiencing breadth of repertoire will be a key philosophical component of Chamber Choir, high quality choral performance will drive the methodology. In that regard, student directors, through collaboration with the instructor, will prepare and conduct one selection in an authentic performance setting.

title: Flute Choir
description: Applied ensemble for flute students at the graduate level.

title: Music Fundamentals
description: A foundational course in music focused on the rudiments of music and tonal theory. Topics include clefs, pitches, rhythm, meter, scales, keys, intervals, and triads. The class is open to non-majors if space permits.

title: Integrating Music in the Early Childhood and Elementary Classroom
description: This course establishes a foundation of basic skills in music and prepares prospective classroom teachers to integrate music in the early childhood and elementary classroom.

title: Theory I: Voice Leading
description: Study of the fundamental elements of music theory, including rhythm, melody, consonance, dissonance, and functional harmony. We explore these through counterpoint exercises in two, three, and four voices. The prerequisite for this course is MUS 100 .  A placement exam is available. Contact the department for details.

title: Theory II: Diatonic Harmony
description: Study of the diatonic principles of the common-practice era, including voice leading in triads and seventh chords, basic harmonic syntax, and contrapuntal embellishment. We explore these through four-part writing, analysis, harmonic dictation, keyboard exercises, and sight singing.

title: Intro To Listening
description: This course surveys the history of Western music.  Musical styles and genres from the 1600s through present-day will be covered.  This course will not only examine the music of the baroque, classical, and romantic periods, but will also delve into the musical worlds of musical theater, film, jazz, the mainstream, as well as other innovative ideas of the 20th and 21st centuries.  In addition, this course will focus on teaching students to become active listeners rather than passive hearers in order to discover the beauty, intricacy, and greatness of music from all styles and eras.  Students will have the opportunity to apply learned listening concepts by attending live concert events during the semester.  No musical experience or training is necessary for this course.

title: Honors Introduction to Listening
description: This course will explore one of the fundamental modes of aesthetic expression, music. Presupposing no formal training on the part of the students, we will explore aural skills and tools for recognition through study of music fundamentals and masterworks of Euro-American art music from the Baroque era (1600s) to the present. At the same time, students will be given an overview of the development of Western art music, and critically think how that development relates to larger issues of culture and society.  This course is a combination of both online work for which the students are responsible outside of class time, as well as in-class work during the class meeting times each week.  Please pay close attention to the online course deadlines as we move through the semester.

title: Songwriting I: Analyses and Techniques
description: Musical components of songs will be analyzed and compared with songwriting literature; students will sing and perform songs and song concepts on musical instruments.

title: Songwriting II: Application
description: Songs containing melody, harmony, lyrics, and a title will be composed.

title: Oboe Reedmaking
description: Independent laboratory instruction in oboe reedmaking. Up to four semesters of MUS 161 may be counted toward the degree.

title: Intro Music Therapy
description: The purpose of this course is to study music as a human behavior and gain an overview of the use of music with various age groups and populations.

title: Music Therapy Practicum
description: Supervised music therapy clinical work with older adults and observation of music therapy sessions in the community.

title: Intro to Music Education
description: Techniques associated with teaching music in schools. Development of classroom management skills and reflection on the role of the music educator in contemporary classrooms.

title: Theory III: Chromatic Harmony
description: Study of the chromatic principles of the common-practice era, including tonicization and modulation, sequences, advanced figuration, and mixture. We explore these through four-part writing, analysis, harmonic dictation, keyboard exercises, and sight singing.

title: Theory IV: Advanced Harmony
description: Study of advanced chromatic principles of the common-practice era, including chromaticized sequences, advanced mixture, chromatic modulation, and equal division of the octave. We explore these through four-part writing, analysis, harmonic dictation, composition exercises, and sight singing.

title: Composition I
description: Basic techniques of musical composition.

title: Composition II
description: A continuation of Composition I. Basic techniques of musical composition.

title: Jazz Piano Jazz Theory I
description: Introduction to theoretic principles of jazz and application on the piano, including definition and applications of chords and scales, progressions, chord substitution principles. Offered in the fall semesters of even-numbered years.

title: Jazz Piano And Theory II
description: Continuation of MUS 228 . Focus on modes, synthetic scales, altered extensions, and other extended techniques of the jazz idiom. Offered in the spring semesters of odd-numbered years.

title: Improvisation I
description: Theoretical and applied study of the tonal and rhythmic elements used in developing improvisational skills in jazz, rock, and pop media. Offered in the fall semesters of odd-numbered years.

title: Improvisation II
description: Continuation of MUS 230 . Designed to develop advanced improvisational skills in the jazz idiom. Offered in the spring semesters of even-numbered years.

title: Piano Pedagogy
description: Study of teaching principles, methods, and materials; includes microteaching and observation. Offered in alternate spring semesters.

title: Music In World Cultures
description: Study of music in non-Western traditions (Africa, India, Japan, Indonesia, etc.).

title: Music History I
description: History of music from the ancient Greeks through the end of the 17th century, with emphasis on critical analysis of musical, historical, and cultural contexts. Offered in the fall semester.

title: Music History II
description: History of music in the 18th and early 19th centuries, with emphasis on critical analysis of musical, historical, and cultural contexts. Offered in the spring semester.

title: Diction For Singers I
description: Introduction to the International Phonetic Alphabet; study and practice of Italian and English diction. Offered in alternate years.

title: Diction For Singers II
description: Study and practice of German and French diction. Offered in alternate years.

title: Repertoire & Skill Proficiency in Music Therapy
description: The purpose of this course is to learn the wealth of folk, popular, and recreational music of the main segments of American culture, as well as develop polished singing and accompanying skills needed for competent performance as a music therapist. Students will also be introduced to other music therapy interventions.

title: Music Therapy Techniques
description: Learn to develop specific therapeutic experiences (singing, movement, rhythm, relaxation, lyric analysis, lyric rewrite, etc.) for each age group (infancy through older adults) as well as for specific populations. By the end of the semester students will have acquired the skills necessary for MUS 382 .

title: Music Therapy Practicum
description: The purpose of this course is to offer the music therapy student experience in facilitating music therapy sessions at a local agency which serves a specific population and to observe other populations in music therapy sessions. Across the two semesters students will develop group leadership skills in singing and accompanying with guitar, build a repertoire of appropriate songs for this population, and practice appropriate interaction skills with those served, their families, the staff, classmates, and the professor.

title: Observation and Orientation to Music Therapy
description: The purpose of this course is to apply principles of human behavior to clinical settings, with an emphasis on measuring client outcomes.

title: Readings in Music Theory
description: An introduction to current trends in music theory with an emphasis on close reading and analytical writing.

title: 16th-Century Counterpoint
description: Practical study, through analysis and composition, of vocal polyphony as developed in the 16th century.

title: 18th-Century Counterpoint I
description: The 18-century style of counterpoint as developed by J.S. Bach.

title: Form And Analysis
description: Complete survey of the forms of composition, beginning with phrase and period and covering large forms such as rondo, sonata, concerto, and fugue. Writing proficiency is required for a passing grade in this course.  A student who does not write with the skill normally required of an upper-division student will not earn a passing grade, no matter how well the student performs in other areas of the course.

title: Pro Tools Essentials
description: Pro Tools essential is an introductory course guiding the student through the art of digital audio recording. Founded in the industry-standard Pro Tools, the students will be guided through the process of recording in a Digital Audio Workstation (DAW). Focus will be on the basic applications of processes in digital recording that are applicable to all DAW in the industry.

title: Arranging
description: Basic jazz arranging techniques for small ensembles. Offered in the fall semester.

title: Intermediate Arranging
description: Continuation of MUS 310 (Arranging). Emphasis on writing for the big band and mixed media instrumentation, focusing on extended arranging techniques. Offered in the spring semester.

title: Mus Managemt Activity
description: Practical consideration of all aspects of concert production and administration for musicians and music business people.

title: Composition III
description: Projects in musical composition.

title: Composition IV
description: Projects in musical composition.

title: Music History III
description: In this course, students engage with a cultural and analytic survey of Western art music in Europe and the United States from 1800 to present. The survey is intended to promote an understanding of this musical heritage as well as to illuminate some of the cultural and historical causes of artistic change. As a Writing course, it emphasizes writing in the discipline; as a U.S./Global Citizenship course, it equips students to responsibly navigate the socio-cultural contexts of music in national and global contexts. Students engage with representative works, explore primary sources, investigate concepts that have shaped the institutions and practices of classical music, and critically consider the concept of canonicity. Driving questions include: How are musical works products of the social, political, religious, and ideological trends of their time? How did conceptual developments and institutional infrastructures supporting Western art music change during the periods examined? What roles have minorities played in the history of Western art music?.

title: Instrumentation
description: A survey of instrumental strengths, abilities, and weaknesses. Basic instrumentation and writing for all ensembles and orchestra. This course encourages work that overlaps with other programs of study, such as composition, performance, audio engineering, and music production.

title: Arts Venues Management
description: Through course readings, lectures, guest presentations, and hands-on experience students will learn the roles and operations of arts and music venues.   This class is a combination of practical work and classroom lectures.  The class will be held at several local venues, the primary one being the Tuscaloosa Amphitheater.   Other venues include Tuscaloosa River Market, Mildred Westervelt Warner Transportation Museum, Bama Theatre and UA venues.

title: Studies Special Literat
description: Thorough study of topics not included in the curriculum. Offered according to demand.

title: Orchestration
description: A survey of instrumental strengths, abilities, and weaknesses. Special emphasis on the art and science of writing for larger ensembles. This course encourages work that overlaps with other programs of study; particularly composition, performance, audio engineering, and music production.

title: Jazz Symposium
description: No description available .

title: Accompanying Class
description: Survey of accompanying techniques and of the standard repertoire in the vocal, string, and wind areas. Experience is gained through assigned projects and/or assigned studio accompanying. Offered in the spring semester of even-numbered years.

title: Introduction to Piano Technology
description: This course introduces students to the history, design, maintenance, and practical care of the piano. Topics include the evolution of the piano, upright and grand piano construction, tuning practices, regulation, repairs, and long-term care. Students will learn through a combination of lectures, demonstrations, and hands-on practice sessions. Special attention will be given to tuning methods, technical work, and the role of the modern piano technician. Students will complete written assignments, quizzes, and applied projects to demonstrate their understanding of piano technology in both historical and practical contexts. The course strongly encourages students to use artificial intelligence responsibly as a tool for gathering information, supporting critical thinking, and enriching their research.

title: Intro Recording Techniq
description: Exploration of the basics of the art of sound recording. Emphasis on the mixing console, microphone placement, effects, recording basic tracks, and mixdown.

title: Advanced Recording/Mixdown Techniques
description: This course is a continuation of MUS 340 , Introduction to Recording Techniques.  Focus will be placed on recording techniques with an emphasis on the mix down process. Students will work in a hands-on environment to apply techniques discussed in class, including but not limited to: extended recording techniques; use of plugins and outboard gear; mastering; etc.

title: String Pedagogy
description: No description available .

title: Fund Of Conducting
description: Introduction to the basic techniques of conducting with emphasis on the understanding and control of music materials. Offered in the fall semester.

title: Introduction to Strings, Winds and Percussion
description: Group instruction in and exposure to techniques for working with wind, string, and percussion instruments.

title: Teaching Strings
description: Class instruction in the fundamentals of playing and teaching orchestral string instruments.

title: Teaching Flute Single Reeds
description: Class instruction in the fundamentals of playing and teaching flute and single reeds.

title: Teaching Double Reeds
description: Class instruction in the fundamentals of playing and teaching oboe and bassoon.

title: Teaching Brass Instruments
description: Class instruction in the fundamentals of playing and teaching brass instruments.

title: Teaching Percussion
description: Class instruction in the fundamentals of playing and teaching percussion.

title: Teaching Voice for Instrumentalists
description: Class instruction in the fundamentals of vocal production and teaching singing.

title: Vocal Methods
description: Vocal Methods prepares students to apply healthy and effective vocal technique to the choral rehearsal through practical pedagogy. Students explore strategies for building sound, teaching independence and literacy, addressing changing voices, and sequencing instruction for voices of all ages.

title: General Music Classroom Instruments
description: Class instruction in the fundamentals of playing and teaching instruments and sound sources commonly used in general music classroom settings.

title: Clinical Experiences in Music Education
description: Development of skills and understanding vital to the music educator as a manager of classroom discipline, facilitator of learning, and primary agent of student growth in the music environment. Students engage with research, theory, and practice related to effective teaching and learning, classroom management, motivation, and developmental readiness.

title: Assessment in Music Education
description: Overview of assessment and evaluation techniques in music education. This course prepares students to use multiple methods of assessment to engage learners, monitor learner progress, and guide classroom decision making.

title: Music in Childhood
description: Overview of elementary general music curricular approaches and pedagogy. This course prepares students to use multiple methods and varied materials in teaching sequential and comprehensive musicianship to children in grades P–6.

title: Legal Issues in the Arts
description: This course will explore a broad range of legal topics pertinent to the arts, including: copyright, trademark, contracts, management of employees, union issues, advertising and 501 (c)(3) non-profit status.

title: Introduction to the Psychology Music
description: Basic study of the ear and hearing; research methods and clinical applications of research in music therapy; and the psychological, sociological, and physiological processes involved in musical behavior.

title: Music Therapy Practicum
description: Supervised music therapy experience in local facilities.

title: Film Scoring I
description: A study of the art of scoring music for films. Emphasis will be placed on the short jingle and the technical aspects of film scoring. Offered in the fall semester.

title: Film Scoring II
description: Continuation of MUS 401 . Emphasis will be placed on writing the extended film score by analysis of psychological impact, orchestrational techniques, etc. Offered in the spring semester.

title: 18th-Century Counterpoint II
description: The analysis and writing of fugue, variation, ground-bass, and canon.

title: Studio Tech Arrang Orchs
description: Continued development of arranging and orchestrating techniques in both jazz and commercial settings, with an emphasis on the studio orchestra, MIDI instrumentation, and vocal groups.

title: Keyboard Techniques
description: Applied survey of figured bass, choral open-score reading, transposition, and harmonization. Offered in the fall semesters of odd-numbered years.

title: Marketing the Arts
description: Marketing the Arts will provide students with a general overview of communications including public relations, advertising, marketing and new media. Through readings, discussions, quizzes, an examination and a final group presentation students will develop an understanding of marketing practices involved in promoting an arts organization.  Emphasis will be on not-for-profit organizations, community theatres and symphony orchestras.

title: Electronic Music I
description: A survey of seminal works featuring electronic elements. Mastering the principles of recording, editing, signal processing, mixing, mastering, MIDI, sampling, and sound design. This course encourages work that overlaps with other programs of study; particularly composition, performance, audio engineering, and music production.

title: Electronic Music II
description: A continued survey of seminal works featuring electronic elements. Instruction in the design and utilization of sound-generating computer programs for digital synthesis, MIDI, and advanced techniques associated with recording and producing audio. This course encourages work that overlaps with other programs of study; particularly, composition, performance, and audio engineering degrees.

title: Special Topics in Music Theory
description: Advanced course in music theory; topics may vary according to instructor.

title: Special Topics in Composition
description: Advanced course in music composition; topics may vary according to instructor.

title: Schenkerian Analysis
description: Study of pitch structure in tonal compositions as revealed by Heinrich Schenker and his followers.

title: Composition V
description: Projects in composition.

title: Composition VI
description: Projects in composition.

title: Analysis of 20th-Century Music
description: Survey of a variety of technical approaches to composition in the 20th century and such organizing techniques as harmony, voice leading, textural design, form, and orchestration.

title: Studies In Special Lit
description: Thorough study of topics not included in the curriculum. Offered according to demand.

title: Applied Recording
description: Applied recording is an individualized study of the art of recording, mixing, mastering, and production of an entire recording project. Based upon needs of the majors as determined by the professor and student, an individualized program of study for the semester will be designed to assist the student and guide them through topics needed for a comprehensive education in recording/mixing engineering.

title: Popular Music: Critical Views
description: This course uses recent interviews with popular music artists as a way in to interrogating current social issues through music. Do these artists create their music with an agenda? In response to this question, “Popular Music: Critical Views” allows students to engage in contemporary topics via popular music, and it emphasizes a plurality of perspectives—both of the students and the artists. Students draw on their diverse backgrounds to participate in dialogues of critical inquiry through guided discussion. Students use the innovative and open-access analytical tool Auralayer for the analysis of texture and instrumentation.

title: Advanced Piano Pedagogy
description: Advanced Piano Pedagogy focused on the teaching of the intermediate and advanced pre-college piano student. Topics covered will include learning styles; methods of teaching advanced rhythm and reading; creating a healthy technical approach for advanced repertoire; intermediate and advanced pedagogical solo material; teaching stylistic interpretation and musicianship; and issues of motivation in advancing students.

title: Vocal Pedagogy
description: Introduction to the concepts, methods, and materials essential to effective teaching of the singing voice. Offered in alternate years.

title: Teaching Choral Music Publ Sch
description: The scope, organization, and administration of choral programs in public schools. Includes analysis of teaching techniques and materials appropriate to specific levels of development. Offered in the fall semester.

title: Tchng Instrumental Music Pub S
description: Problems encountered in the development of instrumental programs in public schools; analysis of beginning method books for heterogeneous and homogeneous groups; and development of a course of study to guide the new teacher. Offered in the fall semester.

title: History/Literature Jazz
description: History of the development of jazz to the present, from its African roots to its American florescence. Particular attention is paid to early styles and forms.

title: Lit Of The Piano
description: Survey of the important solo repertoire from Bach through Schubert. Examination of the development of keyboard technique from the time of the harpsichord through the time of the early piano. Offered in the fall semesters of even-numbered years.

title: Lit Of The Piano
description: Survey of the important solo repertoire from the early Romantic era through the present. Examination of the development of piano technique from Chopin and Liszt through Cage and Crumb. Offered in the spring semesters of odd-numbered years.

title: String Literature
description: Study of the standard repertoire for bowed instruments, from the Baroque period to the present.

title: Song Literature
description: Survey of the performance criteria and historical significance of the solo song literature of the United States, the British Isles, Germany, Austria, France, and Italy. Performance and class participation are required. Offered in alternate years.

title: Song Literature
description: Survey of a variety of technical approaches to composition in the 20th century and such organizing techniques as harmony, voice leading, textural design, form, and orchestration. Offered in the fall semesters.

title: Music in Therapy I
description: Study of mental health diagnoses as well as various therapeutic techniques and systems in music therapy using a hypothetical mode of inquiry. Writing proficiency is required for a passing grade in this course.  A student who does not write with the skill normally required of an upper-division student will not earn a passing grade, no matter how well the student performs in other areas of the course.

title: Music Therapy II
description: The purpose of this course is to study the needs of the aging population as well as medical diagnoses of all age groups, including evidence-based practice in music therapy.

title: Music Therapy III
description: Music therapy for children, adolescents, and adults with disabilities in self-contained and inclusive settings.

title: Music Therapy Practicum
description: The purpose of this course is to provide music therapy students supervised experience in facilitating music therapy sessions at local facilities throughout the community. This encompasses assessment, program planning, implementation, documentation, and evaluation of music therapy services.

title: Music Therapy Internship
description: Music therapy internship through a university affiliation or national roster site. Student must accumulate 1200 clinical training hours, in which 900 hours or more take place during the internship.

title: Raising Money Dev Arts
description: Theories and techniques of securing funds for nonprofit organizations, including donor cultivation and grant preparation.

title: Choral Conducting
description: Continuation of MUS 351 with emphasis on the problem of the choral ensemble. A study of rehearsal technique, vocal problems, diction, score analysis, style, and choral literature. Offered in the spring semester.

title: Marching Band Design and Methods
description: This course is designed to provide students with both theoretical and practical knowledge of marching band design and methodologies. Through this course, students will explore the principles of planning, design, execution, and administration of a successful marching band program. The course is intended for students interested in developing skills in managing all aspects of a marching band, including logistical planning, creative design, leadership development, and technical execution.

title: Band Conducting
description: Continuation of MUS 351 with emphasis on the problem of the wind ensemble. A study of baton technique, score reading, rehearsal procedure, and band literature. Offered in the spring semester.

title: Internship in Music Education
description: Full time, supervised teaching experience in the area of music education Students will intern in a school setting covering elementary grades P–6 and secondary grades 6–12.

title: Music Admin Internship
description: A minimum of one semester of internship with the following recommended host organizations: (1) a performing arts organization, (2) a community, state, or regional arts council, (3) a performing arts venue, (4) a musical festival or (5) a reputable recording studio.  The internship must have the approval of and will be under the supervision of the Music Administration Coordinator.

title: Capstone Senior Project
description: Required of all BA students in music (excepting music administration): Capstone experience consisting of a substantial paper or project on a topic approved by the faculty advisor and completed in consultation with the advisor and examined by a faculty mentor, or a supervised practicum arranged through the advisor.

title: Independent Research in Music
description: Independent study of music at the undergraduate level.

title: Class Voice Beginning
description: No description available .

title: Early Chamber Ensembles
description: Early Chamber Ensembles is a small ensemble elective course open to undergraduate music majors and minors upon audition. The Early Chamber Ensembles allow students to explore chamber music on period and modern instruments, learn historical playing and singing techniques, and hone interpretive skills in various Baroque and Classical styles.

title: Saxophone Ensemble
description: Applied Ensemble. This ensemble is open by audition to all undergraduate University of Alabama students.

title: Campus Orchestra
description: The Campus Orchestra is a community-building partnership between the University of Alabama School of Music and its affiliated Community Music School (CMS). The Campus Orchestra is open to UA students, motivated pre-college students, and post-college adults. This ensemble is conducted by a graduate conducting major with support by School of Music faculty members. Acceptance and seating are by audition.

title: Tuscaloosa-UA Community Chorus
description: The Tuscaloosa-UA Community Chorus is an auditioned choral ensemble that unites members of the University of Alabama School of Music and the greater Tuscaloosa community. This SATB choral ensemble celebrates and embraces the diversity of humanity through music in the form of solo choral performances and collaborative choral-orchestral performances. Members of this ensemble will engage with a wide variety of choral repertoire styles, composers, and historical eras representative of the diverse world in which our community exists.

title: Musician’s Collective
description: Using principles of constructivist learning, students will collaborate in small groups to create original music, arrangements of existing music, and performances in desired and blended music genres. Collaborations will also involve preparing, promoting, and producing music in live and videographic formats. All instruments, voice types, digital audio workstations (DAWs), and genre preferences are welcome.

title: Trumpet Ensemble
description: Applied performance ensemble for trumpet students at the undergraduate level.

title: Studio Ensemble
description: Studio ensemble is a lab ensemble.  The group will rehearse and perform in the Bryant Jordan Recording Studio. The format will be a recording session. Each rehearsal will have a new piece that the students must learn and record within the constraints of a standard time-sensitive recording session, a la film score, jingle, CD backing musician, etc.

title: Class Piano I
description: This is the first course in a four-semester sequence of courses ( MUA 121 , 122, 123, 124) designed to develop the keyboard skills which are essential for all musicians. Most degree programs offered by the School of Music require competency in keyboard skills. The topics covered in Class Piano are designed to equip one to use the piano as a resource in all manners of music-life.  Topics can include (but are not limited to) the following: sight-reading, learning repertoire, scales and arpeggios, transposition, harmonization, composition, improvisation, fundamentals of various styles (classical, jazz, blues, mainstream), advanced chord use, song creation, chord chart and lead sheet reading, accompaniment, playing by ear, open-score reading, etc. Class Piano is designed for music majors. Non-music majors with prior music knowledge and experience may enroll in the course with instructor permission as seats are available.

title: Class Piano II
description: This is the second course in a four-semester sequence of courses ( MUA 121 , 122, 123, 124) designed to develop the keyboard skills which are essential for all musicians. Most degree programs offered by the School of Music require competency in keyboard skills. The topics covered in Class Piano are designed to equip one to use the piano as a resource in all manners of music-life.  Topics can include (but are not limited to) the following: sight-reading, learning repertoire, scales and arpeggios, transposition, harmonization, composition, improvisation, fundamentals of various styles (classical, jazz, blues, mainstream), advanced chord use, song creation, chord chart and lead sheet reading, accompaniment, playing by ear, open-score reading, etc. Class Piano is designed for music majors.  Non-music majors with prior music knowledge and experience may enroll in the course with instructor permission as seats are available.

title: Class Piano III
description: This is the third course in a four-semester sequence of courses ( MUA 121 , 122, 123, 124) designed to develop the keyboard skills which are essential for all musicians. Most degree programs offered by the School of Music require competency in keyboard skills. The topics covered in Class Piano are designed to equip one to use the piano as a resource in all manners of music-life.  Topics can include (but are not limited to) the following: sight-reading, learning repertoire, scales and arpeggios, transposition, harmonization, composition, improvisation, fundamentals of various styles (classical, jazz, blues, mainstream), advanced chord use, song creation, chord chart and lead sheet reading, accompaniment, playing by ear, open-score reading, etc. Class Piano is designed for music majors.  Non-music majors with prior music knowledge and experience may enroll in the course with instructor permission as seats are available.

title: Class Piano IV
description: This is the fourth course in a four-semester sequence of courses ( MUA 121 , 122, 123, 124) designed to develop the keyboard skills which are essential for all musicians. Most degree programs offered by the School of Music require competency in keyboard skills. The topics covered in Class Piano are designed to equip one to use the piano as a resource in all manners of music-life.  Topics can include (but are not limited to) the following: sight-reading, learning repertoire, scales and arpeggios, transposition, harmonization, composition, improvisation, fundamentals of various styles (classical, jazz, blues, mainstream), advanced chord use, song creation, chord chart and lead sheet reading, accompaniment, playing by ear, open-score reading, etc. Class Piano is designed for music majors. Non-music majors with prior music knowledge and experience may enroll in the course with instructor permission as seats are available.

title: Chamber Choir
description: The Alabama Chamber Choir is a mixed choral ensemble primarily conducted by advanced University of Alabama undergraduate choral music education students.  Chamber Choir will afford all choristers the opportunity to peruse a broad spectrum of choral ensemble music, most of which will be suitable for programming in a traditional public school choral music setting.  While experiencing breadth of repertoire will be a key philosophical component of Chamber Choir, high quality choral performance will drive the methodology. In that regard, student directors, through collaboration with the instructor, will prepare and conduct one selection in an authentic performance setting.

The Campus Orchestra is open to UA students, motivated pre-college students, and post-college adults. This ensemble is conducted by a graduate conducting major with support by School of Music faculty members. Acceptance and seating are by audition.

title: Tuscaloosa-UA Community Chorus
description: The Tuscaloosa-UA Community Chorus is an auditioned choral ensemble that unites members of the University of Alabama School of Music and the greater Tuscaloosa community. This SATB choral ensemble celebrates and embraces the diversity of humanity through music in the form of solo choral performances and collaborative choral-orchestral performances. Members of this ensemble will engage with a wide variety of choral repertoire styles, composers, and historical eras representative of the diverse world in which our community exists.

Collaborations will also involve preparing, promoting, and producing music in live and videographic formats. All instruments, voice types, digital audio workstations (DAWs), and genre preferences are welcome.

title: Foundatn Music Education
description: Investigation of the purposes and functions of music education from antiquity to the present. Philosophical foundations and a chronological survey of historical issues related to the inclusion of music in general education will also be discussed.

title: General Music:  Approaches and Practices
description: This course examines curricular approaches and contemporary practices in general music education.  Students explore important questions about the purposes and characteristics of general music programs and how those programs meet the needs of diverse learners and communities.  Students develop critical and reflective understandings of general music education within the context of current challenges and opportunities facing the field.

title: Choral Techn Materials
description: Organization, rehearsal, programming and performance of choral groups.

title: Instrml Techn Materls
description: Organization, rehearsal, programming and performance of instrumental groups.

title: Mus Ed Curr Theory Dev
description: Analysis of music curricula and study of the development process.

title: Research in the History and Aesthetics of Music Education
description: This course will address various techniques into the history and aesthetics of music education.

title: Curriculum Projects In Mued
description: The design, proposal, implementation, evaluation, and reporting of a curriculum project or an improved instructional procedure in music. Independent study.

title: Intro Research In Mued
description: Introduction to research study in music education.

title: Introduction to Graduate Studies in Music Education
description: Introduction designed to prepare student for the application of scholarly thinking to topics in the areas of music psychology, teacher training, and music education.

title: Marching Band Techniques
description: Fundamentals of marching, maneuvering, and preparation and presentation of formations.

title: Clinical Exper. in MUE
description: Part-time supervised teaching experience in secondary instrumental music education.  Four-twelve hours weekly for 14 weeks in Tuscaloosa area public schools.

title: Seminar In Music Education
description: Study of special topics in music education.

title: Practicum Music Education
description: Supervised teaching experience in the area of specialization (instrumental or vocal and elementary), from nursery school through grade 12.

title: Non-Thesis Research
description: No description available .

title: Thesis Research
description: No description available .

title: Doctor of Philosophy Diagnostic/Admission Exam
description: Examination required prior to completion of no more than six hours of study for admission to a Ph.D. program in music education.

title: Curriculum Projects Mued
description: The design, proposal, implementation, evaluation, and reporting of a curriculum project or an improved instructional procedure in music. Independent study.

title: Adv Research Music Educ
description: The design, implementation and reporting of research studies that illustrate a variety of methodological and statistical plans for research in music education.

title: Non-Dissertatn Research
description: No description available .

title: Dissertation Research
description: No description available .

title: Basic Skills In Music
description: Basic music-reading skills developed through singing and playing of instruments. The course is designed for the prospective elementary school teacher and is a prerequisite for MUE 385 .

title: Intro to Music Education
description: Techniques associated with teaching music in the public schools.  Development of classroom management skills and reflection on the role of the music educator in contemporary classrooms.

title: Intro To Winds, Str, Per
description: Group instruction in and exposure to techniques for working with wind, string, and percussion instruments. This survey course includes lecture, demonstration, and hands-on participation.

title: Teach String/Percu Instr
description: Group instruction in and exposure to techniques of teaching and performing on string, and percussion instruments. This survey course includes lecture, demonstration, and hands-on participation.

title: Evaluatn Teaching Learning Mus
description: Survey of standardized instruments and evaluation techniques in music.

title: Tchg Flute Single Reeds
description: Instructional techniques and materials for teaching flute and single reeds.

title: Teaching Double Reeds
description: Instructional techniques and materials for teaching oboe and bassoon.

title: Teach Brass Instruments
description: Designed to provide the instrumental music education major with skills necessary to teach beginning, intermediate, and advanced students of brass wind instruments.

title: Teaching Percussion II
description: Instructional techniques and materials for teaching intermediate and advanced players.

title: Vocal Methods
description: Examination of vocal pedagogy, diction, and solo vocal literature appropriate for teaching students in a public-school setting.

title: Vocal Methods Lab
description: Examination of the vocal instrument and teaching strategies for working with young voices.

title: Teaching Music in the Early Childhood and Elementary Classroom
description: This course prepares future classroom teachers for engaging children in meaningful musical experiences in the early childhood and elementary classroom.  Students explore music and movement development in children, and music and movement's role in children's development.  Participants design and facilitate musical experiences including preforming (singing and playing), responding (listening, moving and describing), and creating (composing and improvising).  Participants examine and create curricular experiences to connect and integrate music across the curriculum.

title: Elem School Music Program
description: Comprehensive coverage of the music curriculum in the elementary school, including current methodologies. Teaching techniques and materials are demonstrated and applied in field experiences. Writing proficiency within this discipline is required for a passing grade in this course.

title: Clinical Experience In Mue
description: Campus- and field-based experiences in public-school music education at several levels. Microteaching and development of teaching techniques are included.

title: Instrumental Curriculum and Instruction
description: The content of this course includes techniques associated with curriculum design, materials selection, rehearsing, and teaching instrumental music when in secondary music rehearsals. A major component of this course is the development of observable professional behaviors that most successful band and orchestra directors exhibit daily. The course emphasizes rehearsal techniques and the application of pedagogical skills to the actual rehearsal situation. Score preparation, rehearsal planning, score study, program design, gestural techniques, and concert preparation are topics for lectures.

title: Choral Curriculum and Instruction
description: The content of this course includes techniques associated with curriculum design, materials selection, rehearsing, and teaching choral music when in secondary music rehearsals. A major component of this course is the development of observable professional behaviors that most successful choir directors exhibit daily. The course emphasizes rehearsal techniques and the application of pedagogical skills to the actual rehearsal situation. Score preparation, rehearsal planning, score study, program design, gestural techniques, and concert preparation are topics for lectures.

title: Marching Band Techniques
description: Fundamentals of marching, maneuvering, and preparation and presentation of formations.

title: Internship Music Educ
description: Full time supervised teaching experience in the area of specialization (instrumental or vocal).

title: Chart Your Own Path
description: A freshman compass course familiarizing first-year students with University requirements and resources. Course also covers integrative learning across the liberal arts to maximize opportunities in undergraduate programs. Encouraged for undecided students and those considering an Interdisciplinary Studies major through New College.

title: Inclusive Leadership Through Sustained Dialogue
description: In an increasingly polarized world, being an engaged citizen requires skills for resolving the conflicts inherent in a diverse democratic society. Sustained Dialogue (SD) is a five-stage dialogue-to-action model that focuses on transforming change-blocking relationships in order to address issues of social and political differences more effectively. Students will learn the principles and practices of Sustained Dialogue, and they will develop skills for participating in and moderating positive, productive exchanges on challenging topics.

title: Outdoor Leadership and Service
description: This experiential, service-learning seminar will focus on biodiversity and introduce students to our local environment, local environmental issues, related global environmental issues, and UA’s environmental faculty.

title: Sexuality and Society
description: American society today features more cultural acceptance and legal protection than ever before for sexual and gender diversity, but we don’t always know how to live out these changing norms and how to talk about controversial sexual material in the public sphere.  This course engages debates around sexuality as central to human behavior and to social structures, in both America and the world.  It provides an overview of the “new sexual revolution” and the growing interdisciplinary field of sexuality studies.  The course adopts a consent-based model of sexual wellbeing.  Its approach is sex-positive—aiming toward sexual justice, responsibility, and pleasure—within a classroom that functions as an open and affirming space for discussion and learning.  Students gain the knowledge, critical thinking skills, and cultural competence to evaluate for themselves issues of sexuality in society.

title: Technology and Society
description: Across the globe today, there is a growing interdependence between human beings and advanced digital, computing, biotech, robotics, and artificial intelligence technologies. Many are debating what impact this increasingly close relationship and intertwining is having on human interpersonal relationships and behavior, the future of work and employment, and our social and political structures and institutions. This course engages these debates about recent advanced technologies to consider how they have and continue to impact both human behavior and social structures, in the United States and around the world. It provides an overview of recent technological advancements in computing, artificial intelligence, autonomous systems, biotechnology, robotics, and immersive systems and the interdisciplinary examinations of these advances from sociological, psychological, ethical, philosophical, and political perspectives. Students will gain the knowledge, critical thinking skills, and cultural competence to evaluate and analyze for themselves the issues surrounding the integral adoption of these advanced technologies in contemporary society.

title: Idea Lab
description: Idea Lab is an interdisciplinary research incubator that challenges and empowers students to engage in a collaborative, semester-long research project. In addition to learning best practices in interdisciplinary research, students will learn broadly applicable research skills to approach problems or questions from a range of perspectives.

title: Recreation For Life
description: In this course (designed by the student and his or her advisor using the out-of-class learning contract), the student agrees to participate in some form of physical activity that might result in a lifelong interest. These include jogging, swimming, cycling, etc. This course will frequently include a reading requirement relating to the activity.

title: Perspectives in the Humanities
description: Introductory course in the humanities (art, literature, music, etc.) with a focus on problem solving, risk taking and communication. Human behavior and writing skills are stressed.

title: Creativity
description: This interdisciplinary seminar uses creativity as an organizing principle. Emphasizing history and appreciation rather than performance, this course explores human culture and consciousness through reading, writing, the arts, projects, studios, and discussion.

title: Honors Creativity
description: This honors interdisciplinary seminar uses creativity as an organizing principle. Emphasizing history and appreciation rather than performance, this course explores human culture and consciousness through reading, writing, the arts, projects, studios, and discussion.

title: Perspec. on Env. Literature
description: Considers a number of environmental issues through the use of environmental literature and explores major intellectual and aesthetic ideas conveyed through environmental literature.

title: Digital Making: Creativity and Computers
description: This fine arts seminar uses creative work with digital tools as its organizing principle. Human culture, the creative process, and creative expression are explored through written texts, digital media, research, oral and written reports, journals, and individual and group projects. The class goal is to gain a deeper understanding of creativity, innovation and interdisciplinarity in the arts by using computer technology to make creative work. Students will learn through research, hands-on work on creative projects and collaborative work with peers.

title: Organic Farming
description: An intensive, hands-on course in organic farming taught at a local working farm. Covers the basics of organic farming while also addressing questions about organic versus industrial agriculture models in relation to current environmental problems and solutions.

title: Environmental Studies
description: This course engages students in the study of environmental problems and solutions. It includes an examination of fundamental assumptions about the ethical human-nature relation and of how to value nature. The approach is interdisciplinary, drawing on the humanities, social sciences, and natural sciences.

title: Social Problems and Social Change
description: This seminar explores significant social problems in contemporary society and the complex ways in which social change occurs. Students investigate and seek solutions for current social problems. This course has a 12 hour service-learning component.

title: Social Problems and Social Change
description: This honors seminar explores significant social problems in contemporary society and the complex ways in which social change occurs.  Students investigate and seek solutions to current social problems. This course has a service learning component that may involve 12 service learning hours outside of class time.

title: Interdisciplinary Sciences
description: This seminar demonstrates how the nature of the laboratory experience plays an essential role in the understanding and advancement of science. Several multidisciplinary experiments are performed in geology, chemistry, physics, and biology.

title: Social Issues & Ethics
description: This seminar is designed to develop an awareness of the methodologies and concerns of the social sciences as they relate to ethical inquiry. The primary focus is on the nature of inquiry and models for the analysis of ethical issues confronting the modern world. Students will explore the complexity of moral dilemmas and effective societal responses to competing moral obligations in the areas of health care, socio-economic disparities, affirmative action, immigration, and racism.

title: Outdoor Leadership
description: The goal of this course is to prepare students to successfully and safely plan and lead small group excursions into wilderness, backcountry, and front-country outdoor areas using various outdoor recreational activities as a medium. There will also be an emphasis placed on teaching technique and presentation skills as students will be required to present information to the class.

title: Independent Study
description: A student desiring to pursue an academic interest for which no University class is available may plan their own scholarly, creative or skills-based "course" for graded credit through Out-of-Class Learning. A contract, or agreement, with New College is prepared by the student, in which the student identifies a variety of features of the proposed study: its goals and objectives, the methodology and resources to be employed in the attempt to meet the goals and objectives, and the procedure by which the study will be evaluated upon its completion. The process of preparing the contract should be in cooperation with New College, from which contract forms are available online, and with a faculty member or another authority qualified to assist and assess the study. The number of graded credit hours awarded for Out-of-Class Learning is based, in part, on the breadth or depth of the study and is subject to approval by the contract advisor and director of New College. All New College Independent Study contracts require relevant reading and writing of some kind.

title: Independent Study
description: A student desiring to pursue an internship or other academic interest for which no University class is available may plan an experiential or other "course" for pass-fail credit through Out-of-Class Learning. A contract, or agreement, with New College is prepared by the student, in which the student identifies a variety of features of the proposed internship or course: its goals and objectives, the methodology and resources to be employed in the attempt to meet the goals and objectives, and the procedure by which the study will be evaluated upon its completion. The process of preparing the contract should be in cooperation with New College, from which contract forms are available online, and with a faculty member, internship supervisor, or another authority qualified to assist and assess the study. The number of pass-fail credit hours awarded for Out-of-Class Learning is based, in part, on the breadth or depth of the experience or study and is subject to approval by the contract advisor and director of New College. All New College Independent Study contracts require relevant reading and writing of some kind.

All New College Independent Study contracts require relevant reading and writing of some kind.

title: Independent Study
description: A student desiring to pursue an academic interest for which no University class is available may plan his or her own "course" through Out-of-Class Learning. A contract, or agreement, with New College is prepared by the student, in which the student identifies a variety of features of the proposed study: its goals and objectives, the methodology and resources to be employed in the attempt to meet the goals and objectives, and the procedure by which the study will be evaluated upon its completion. The process of preparing the contract should be in cooperation with the New College office, from which contract forms may be procured, and with a faculty member or other authority qualified to assist and assess the study. Credit hours awarded for Out-of-Class Learning are available, relative to the breadth or depth of the study, and subject to approval of the advisor to the study and director of the New College.

title: Experimental Music
description: This course will introduce you to a variety of theories and practices of musical experimentalism in a global context. We’ll begin by defining what experimental music is – what is the impulse that drives some artists to push outside the realms of their traditions, or even outside what is normally called music? Rather than limiting our view to experimentalism in Western art music, as has largely been the case in surveys of this subject, we will also listen to and study experiments in jazz, rock, hip hop, reggae and other global musics to put the experimentalist aesthetic in a broad historical and cultural context. In addition to critical reading, discussion and listening we will perform experimental music compositions to encourage a “hands-on” engagement with the subject. Writing proficiency is required for a passing grade in this course. A student who does not write with the skill normally required of an upper-division student will not earn a passing grade, no matter how well the student performs in other areas of the course.

title: CIEL Internship
